floorPlan.title: Floor Plan
floorPlan.uploadPrompt: Upload a floor plan to begin
floorPlan.supportedFormats: PNG, JPG, GIF, WEBP, or SVG (max 10MB)
floorPlan.clickToMeasure: Click on the floor plan to take a measurement at that location

samples.title: Samples
samples.clickToStart: Click on the floor plan to start.

calibration.title: Scale Calibration Mode
calibration.instructions: Click two points on the floor plan that represent a known distance (e.g., a wall, doorway, or room dimension), then enter the real-world distance.

setup.calibrateScale: Calibrate the scale to match real distances
setup.wifiInterface: Select a WiFi interface that can scan
setup.readyToStart: Start the survey to begin collecting samples

settings.title: Survey Settings
settings.passiveDesc: Scans all visible WiFi networks at each point
settings.activeDesc: Monitors your current WiFi connection (RSSI, data rate, roaming)
settings.throughputDesc: Runs iperf3 tests for bandwidth, latency, and jitter measurements

scalePanel.title: Scale & Propagation Settings
scalePanel.measureInstructions: Click two points on the floor plan that represent a known distance.
scalePanel.recommendedSamples: Recommended: {{count}} sample points for full coverage

wifi.noAdapter: No wireless adapter detected. Connect a WiFi adapter to perform surveys.
wifi.noAdapterSetup: Setup Mode - No WiFi adapter required
wifi.adapterAvailable: Wireless adapter available but not selected as current interface.
wifi.canStillUpload: You can still upload a floor plan and configure your survey. WiFi adapter is only required when taking samples.
wifi.setupWithoutAdapter: You can upload floor plans, calibrate scale, and configure all survey settings without a WiFi adapter. Connect an adapter when ready to collect samples.
wifi.requiredToStart: WiFi adapter required to start survey

config.title: Scan Configuration

goals.coverageDesc: See where WiFi reaches and measure signal quality. Best for initial site assessment.
goals.connectionDesc: Test actual connection speeds and roaming behavior. Requires connecting to network.
goals.throughputDesc: Measure actual data transfer speeds with iperf3. Requires iperf3 server.
goals.completeDesc: Coverage plus connection quality in one survey. Best for new deployments.
goals.validationDesc: Coverage plus real throughput testing. Best for performance verification.

config.twoRadiosDesc: This survey type uses two radios simultaneously - one for passive scanning and one for active testing. Ensure you have two WiFi adapters connected.
config.multiAdapterTip: With multiple adapters, you can run passive scanning and active testing simultaneously for a single-pass comprehensive survey.
config.singlePassTip: Configure both adapters for a single-pass survey: one for passive scanning, one for active testing.

import.title: Import AirMapper Survey
import.description: Import a NetAlly AirMapper .amp file to use its floor plan, calibration, and AP locations.
import.dropPrompt: Drag and drop an .amp file here
import.orClick: or click to select a file
import.invalidFormat: Invalid file format. Please select an .amp file.

apPlacement.title: AP Locations
apPlacement.label: AP Label
apPlacement.placementMode: Click floor plan to place AP
apPlacement.clickToPlace: Click on the floor plan to place an AP

analysis.title: Survey Analysis
analysis.clickToView: Click to view on floor plan

coverage.deadZonesDesc: Found {{count}} areas with no usable signal (below {{threshold}} dBm)
coverage.deadZonesAction: Consider adding an AP or relocating existing APs to cover these areas
coverage.weakAreasDesc: Found {{count}} areas with weak signal (below {{threshold}} dBm)
coverage.weakAreasAction: Increase AP power, adjust antenna orientation, or add additional APs
coverage.apCountDesc: {{count}} APs have been placed on the floor plan

interference.coChannelDesc: {{value}} APs on channel {{threshold}} at this location
interference.coChannelAction: Reconfigure APs to use non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11 for 2.4GHz)
interference.adjacentChannelAction: Use only non-overlapping channels to reduce interference

security.openNetworksAction: Enable WPA2/WPA3 encryption on all enterprise networks
security.wepNetworksDesc: Found {{count}} network(s) using outdated WEP encryption
security.wepNetworksAction: Upgrade to WPA2/WPA3 - WEP is easily compromised

optimization.overusedChannelsDesc: Channel {{value}} is heavily used across the facility
optimization.overusedChannelsAction: Distribute APs across more channels or use auto-channel selection

overall.healthyDesc: Survey of {{count}} samples shows no critical issues

heatmapDescriptions.rssi: This heatmap shows the Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) in dBm across the surveyed area. Green areas (-30 to -65 dBm) indicate excellent coverage suitable for all applications. Yellow areas (-65 to -75 dBm) indicate acceptable coverage for basic connectivity. Red areas (below -75 dBm) indicate poor coverage that may cause connectivity issues, dropped connections, or slow performance.
heatmapDescriptions.snr: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) measures the difference between signal strength and ambient noise. Higher values indicate cleaner signals. Green areas (25+ dB) provide reliable high-speed connections. Yellow areas (15-25 dB) offer acceptable performance. Red areas (below 15 dB) may experience interference and reduced speeds.
heatmapDescriptions.noise: The noise floor heatmap shows ambient RF interference levels. Lower values (green, below -90 dBm) indicate a clean RF environment. Higher noise levels (red) can degrade WiFi performance even with strong signal strength.
heatmapDescriptions.throughput: Measured throughput shows actual data transfer speeds at each location. This reflects real-world performance accounting for signal quality, interference, and network conditions.
heatmapDescriptions.latency: Latency measures the round-trip time for network packets. Lower latency (green) is essential for real-time applications like voice and video. Higher latency (red) may cause noticeable delays.
heatmapDescriptions.cochannel: Co-channel interference occurs when multiple APs operate on the same channel. This heatmap shows areas where excessive co-channel interference may degrade performance. Fewer APs per channel (green) is better.
heatmapDescriptions.adjacent: Adjacent channel interference occurs between overlapping WiFi channels. This is most common in 2.4 GHz where channels overlap. Green areas have minimal adjacent channel interference.
